title: Transforming data
description: Practice anticipating how a linear transformation of a dataset impacts the mean and standard deviation of the data.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of raw scores on a particular achievement test has a mean of $500$ and a standard deviation of $80$.

**If each score is increased by $25$, what will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of new scores?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

Adding $25$ to every score shifts the distribution of scores higher, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Adding $25$ to every score will increase the mean by $25$.**

##Standard deviation

Adding $25$ to every score doesn't affect measures of spread since the distribution is just shifted higher.

**Adding $25$ to every score won't affect the standard deviation.**

##Answer

Mean: $525$  
Standard deviation: $80$

assessment_items.question_content:
Mr. Dennis gave his class a $20$-question multiple choice quiz. The distribution of raw scores had a mean of $15$ and a standard deviation of $2.5$.

Mr. Dennis is going to multiply each raw score by $5$  so the quiz is worth a total of $100$ points.

**If each score is multiplied by $5$, what will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of new scores?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

Multiplying every score by $5$ increases every score, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Multiplying every score by $5$ will increase the mean by a factor of $5$.**

##Standard deviation

Multiplying every score by $5$ increases the spread between scores, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

**Multiplying every score by $5$ will increase the standard deviation by a factor of $5$.**

##Answer

Mean: $75$  
Standard deviation: $12.5$

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of Americans participated in a study that involved taking their temperatures during exercise in extreme heat. The participants had a mean body temperature of $104^{\circ} \text{F}$ and a standard deviation of $2^{\circ} \text{F}$.

Doctors analyzing the results prefer to work in degrees Celsius, so they convert the data by applying the formula below to each data point:

$^{\circ}\text{C}=\dfrac59(^{\circ}\text{F}-32)$

**If each temperature is converted to degrees Celsius, what will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of new temperatures?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

The first step in the conversion is subtracting $32$ from each measurement. This shifts every data point lower, so measures of center like the mean will also decrease by $32$.

Multiplying by $5/9$ also decreases every measurement, so it will also affect the mean.

$\begin{align} \mu_{^{\circ}\text{C}}&=\dfrac59\left( \mu_{^{\circ}\text{F}}-32 \right) \\
\\
&=\dfrac59(104-32) \\
\\
&=\dfrac59(72) \\
\\
&=40 \end{align}$

##Standard deviation

The first step in the conversion is subtracting $32$ from each measurement. This shifts every data point lower, but it doesn't change the spread between data points, so this does not impact standard deviation.

Multiplying by $5/9$ decreases the spread between measurements, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

$\begin{align} \sigma_{^{\circ}\text{C}}&=\dfrac59\left( \sigma_{^{\circ}\text{F}} \right) \\
\\
&=\dfrac59(2) \\
\\
&\approx1.11 \end{align}$

##Answer

Mean: $40^{\circ}\text{C}$  
Standard deviation: $1.11^{\circ}\text{C}$

assessment_items.question_content:
In Mr. Britt's class the distribution of ages of his students has a mean of $115$ months and a standard deviation of $3.6$ months.

**If no students leave or join the class, what will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of student's ages $4$ months later?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

Adding $4$ months to every age shifts the distribution of ages higher, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Adding $4$ months to every age will increase the mean by $4$.**

##Standard deviation

Adding $4$ months to every age doesn't affect measures of spread since the distribution is just shifted higher.

**Adding $4$ months to every age won't affect the standard deviation.**

##Answer

Mean: $119$ months  
Standard deviation: $3.6$ months

assessment_items.question_content:
Guests at a picnic try to guess the number of candies in a jar. The distribution of guesses has a mean of $85$ candies and a standard deviation of $12$ candies.

Suppose the jar actually holds $105$ candies, and we want to convert each guess to its error—that is, we'll subtract $105$ from each guess to see how far above or how far below the guess was from the correct number.

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of errors?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

To convert each guess to its error, we subtract $105$ from the guess.

Subtractcing $105$ from every value shifts the distribution of values lower, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Subtracting $105$ from every value will decrease the mean by $105$.**

##Standard deviation

Subtracting $105$ candies to every value doesn't affect measures of spread since the distribution is just shifted lower.

**Subtracting $105$ candies from every value won't affect the standard deviation.**

##Answer

Mean: $-20$ candies  
Standard deviation: $12$ candies

assessment_items.question_content:
Yuki weighed several soil samples, each filling a quarter liter measuring cup. The distribution of masses has a mean of $215\,\text{g}$ and a standard deviation of $60\,\text{g}$.

After recording her measurements, Yuki realized that she had forgotten to subtract the mass of the measuring cup, which is $20\,\text{g}$.

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of corrected measurements?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

Subtracting $20$ from every measurement shifts the distribution of measurement lower, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Subtracting $20$ from every measurement will decrease the mean by $20$.**

##Standard deviation

Subtracting $20$ from every measurement doesn't affect measures of spread since the distribution is just shifted lower.

**Subtracting $20$ from every measurement won't affect the standard deviation.**

##Answer

Mean: $195\,\text{g}$  
Standard deviation: $60\,\text{g}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of heights of the twelve-year-old boys on Tim's rugby team had a mean of $150\,\text{cm}$ and a standard deviation of $10\,\text{cm}$.

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of heights in feet?**  
*$1\,\text{cm}$ approximately equals $0.033\,\text{ft}$.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

Multiplying every height by $0.033$ feet per centimeter decreases the number of units of every height, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Multiplying every height by $0.033$ will decrease the mean by a factor of $0.033$.**

##Standard deviation

Multiplying every height by $0.033$ feet per centimeter decreases the spread between number of units of the heights, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

**Multiplying every height by $0.033$ will decrease the standard deviation by a factor of $0.033$.**

##Answer

Mean: $4.95\,\text{ft}$  
Standard deviation: $0.33\,\text{ft}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A school sells pizza slices at the concession stand during each sporting event. The school sells a mean of $200$ slices per event with a standard  deviation of $25$ slices. 

The school spends $\$160$ of fixed costs to buy the pizza before the event and earns a revenue of $\$2$ per slice. They calculate their profit per event according to this formula:

$\text{profit}=\text{[total revenue]}-\text{[total costs]}$

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of profit per event?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

The first step in finding the profit is to calculate the total revenue by multiplying each number of slices by $\$2$. This increases every data point, so it also increases the mean.

The second step is to subtract the fixed costs of $\$160$. This shifts every data point lower, so measures of center like the mean will also decrease by $160$.

$\begin{align} \mu_{\text{profit}}&=2\mu_{\text{slices}}-160 \\
\\
&=2(200)-160 \\
\\
&=400-160 \\
\\
&=240 \end{align}$

##Standard deviation

The first step in finding the profit is to calculate the total revenue by multiplying each number of slices by $\$2$. Multiplying by $2$ increases the spread between data points, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

The second step is to subtract the fixed costs of $\$160$. This shifts every data point lower, but it doesn't change the spread between data points, so this does not impact standard deviation.

$\begin{align} \sigma_{\text{profit}}&=2\left(\sigma_{\text{slices}}\right)\\
\\
&=2(25)\\
\\
&=50 \end{align}$

##Answer

Mean: $\$240$  
Standard deviation: $\$50$

assessment_items.question_content:
Mrs. Fitzgerald rents a stall in a mall where she sells antiques. She earns a mean revenue of $\$500$ per month with a standard deviation of $\$125$. Her stall and new inventory costs her $\$225$ per month and are her only costs. She calculates her profits using this formula:

$\text{profit}=\text{[revenue]}-\text{[costs]}$

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of Mrs. Fitzgerald's profits?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

Subtracting $225$ from every amount of revenue shifts the distribution of profit lower, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Subtracting $225$ from every amount of revenue will decrease the mean by $225$.**

##Standard deviation

Subtracting $225$ from every amount of revenue doesn't affect measures of spread since the distribution is just shifted lower.

**Subtracting $225$ from every amount of revenue won't affect the standard deviation.**

##Answer

Mean: $\$275$  
Standard deviation: $\$125$

assessment_items.question_content:
The amount of cleaning solution a company fills its bottles with has a mean of $33\,\text{fl oz}$ and a standard deviation of $1.5\,\text{fl oz}$. The company advertises that these bottles have $32\,\text{fl oz}$ of cleaning solution.

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of excess cleaning solution, in milliliters?**  
*$(1\,\text{fl oz}$ is approximately $30\,\text{mL}.)$*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

The first step is to find the amount of excess cleaning solution in fluid ounces by subtracting $32$ from each measurement. This shifts every data point lower, so measures of center like the mean will also decrease by $32$.

Multiplying by $30$ also increases every measurement, so it will also affect the mean.

$\begin{align} \mu_{\text{excess mL}}&\approx30\left( \mu_{\text{total fl oz}}-32 \right) \\
\\
&=30(33-32) \\
\\
&=30(1) \\
\\
&=30 \end{align}$

##Standard deviation

The first step is to find the amount of excess cleaning solution in fluid ounces by subtracting $32$ from each measurement. This shifts every data point lower, but it doesn't change the spread between data points, so this does not impact standard deviation.

Multiplying by $30$ increases the spread between measurements, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

$\begin{align} \sigma_{\text{excess mL}}&\approx30\left( \sigma_{\text{total fl oz}} \right)  \\
\\
&=30(1.5) \\
\\
&=45 \end{align}$

##Answer

Mean: $30\,\text{mL}$  
Standard deviation: $45\,\text{mL}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Eitan sells a mean of $\$8000$ worth of merchandise with a standard deviation of $\$1500$ each month.

Each month, Eitan earns a base salary of $\$2000$ plus a commission of $30\%$ of his sales. He calculates his total salary according to this formula:

$\text{[total salary]}=\text{commission}+\text{[base salary]}$

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of Eitan's total monthly salary?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

The first step in finding Eitan's total monthly salary is to calculate the commission by multiplying each amount of sales by $30\%$. This decreases every data point, so it also decreases the mean.

The second step is to add the base salary of $\$2000$. This shifts every data point higher, so measures of center like the mean will also increase by $2000$.

$\begin{align} \mu_{\text{total salary}}&=0.3\mu_{\text{sales}}+2000\\
\\
&=0.3(8000)+2000 \\
\\
&=2400+2000 \\
\\
&=4400 \end{align}$

##Standard deviation

The first step in finding Eitan's total monthly salary is to calculate the commission by multiplying each amount of sales by $30\%$. Multiplying by $0.3$ decreases the spread between measurements, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

The second step is to add the base salary of $\$2000$. This shifts every data point higher, but it doesn't change the spread between data points, so this does not impact standard deviation.

$\begin{align} \sigma_{\text{total salary}}&=0.3\sigma_{\text{sales}}\\
\\
&=0.3(1500)\\
\\
&=450 \end{align}$

##Answer

Mean: $\$4400$  
Standard deviation: $\$450$

assessment_items.question_content:
The employee e-mail accounts in one company are using a mean of $1.07$ gigabytes of memory with a standard deviation of $0.21$ gigabytes.

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of memory usage in megabytes?**  
*$1$ gigabyte equals $1024$ megabytes.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

Multiplying every data point by $1024$ increases every data point, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Multiplying every data point by $1024$ will increase the mean by a factor of $1024$.**

##Standard deviation

Multiplying every data point by $1024$ increases the spread between data points, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

**Multiplying every data point by $1024$ will increase the standard deviation by a factor of $1024$.**

##Answer

Mean: $1096$ megabytes  
Standard deviation: $215$ megabytes

assessment_items.question_content:
The cargo loads that Niels transports in his truck have a mean weight of $42{,}000\,\text{lb}$ with a standard deviation of $1{,}500\,\text{lb}$.

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of weights in kilograms?**  
*$1\,\text{lb}$ approximately equals $0.45\,\text{kg}$.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

Multiplying every measurement by $0.45$ decreases every data point, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Multiplying every measurement by $0.45$ will decrease the mean by a factor of $0.45$.**

##Standard deviation

Multiplying every measurement by $0.45$ decreases the spread between data points, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

**Multiplying every measurement by $0.45$ will decrease the standard deviation by a factor of $0.45$.**

##Answer

Mean: $18{,}900\,\text{kg}$  
Standard deviation: $675\,\text{kg}$

assessment_items.question_content:
In one county, the farms had a mean harvested area of $103$ acres with a standard deviation of $9$ acres.

**What will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of area in square kilometers?**  
*$1$ acre approximately equals $0.004$ square kilometers.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

Multiplying every measurement by $0.004$ decreases every data point, so it will affect measures of center like the mean.

**Multiplying every measurement by $0.004$ will decrease the mean by a factor of $0.004$.**

##Standard deviation

Multiplying every measurement by $0.004$ decreases the spread between data points, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

**Multiplying every measurement by $0.004$ will decrease the standard deviation by a factor of $0.004$.**

##Answer

Mean: $0.412$ square kilometers  
Standard deviation: $0.036$ square kilometers

choices.content:
Mean: $0.412$ square kilometers  
Standard deviation: $0.036$ square kilometers

Mean: $0.412$ square kilometers  
Standard deviation: $9$ square kilometers

Mean: $103$ square kilometers  
Standard deviation: $0.036$ square kilometers

Mean: $103$ square kilometers  
Standard deviation: $9$ square kilometers

assessment_items.question_content:
A refrigerator runs at a mean temperature of $2.5^\circ\text{C}$ with a standard deviation of $0.8^\circ\text{C}$.

The manufacturers want to also sell the refrigerator in countries that use degrees Fahrenheit, so they convert the data by applying the formula below to each data point:

$^\circ\text{F}=\dfrac{9}{5}^\circ\text{C}+32$

**If each temperature is converted to degrees Fahrenheit, what will be the mean and standard deviation of the distribution of the new temperatures?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Mean

The first step in the conversion is multiplying each temperature by $\dfrac{9}{5}$. This increases every data point, so it also increases the mean.

The second step is to add $32$. This shifts every data point higher, so measures of center like the mean will also increase by $32$.

$\begin{align} 
\mu_{^\circ\text{F}}&=\dfrac{9}{5}\mu_{^\circ\text{C}}+32 \\
\\
&=\dfrac{9}{5}(2.5)+32 \\
\\
&=4.5+32 \\
\\
&=36.5\end{align}$

##Standard deviation

The first step in the conversion is multiplying each temperature by $\dfrac{9}{5}$. Multiplying by $\dfrac{9}{5}$ increases the spread between measurements, so it will affect measures of spread like standard deviation.

The second step is to add $32$. This shifts every data point higher, but it doesn't change the spread between data points, so this does not impact standard deviation.

$\begin{align} \sigma_{^\circ\text{F}}&=\dfrac{9}{5}\sigma_{^\circ\text{C}} \\
\\
&=\dfrac{9}{5}(0.8)\\
\\
&=1.44 \end{align}$

##Answer

Mean: $36.5^\circ\text{F}$  
Standard deviation: $1.44^\circ\text{F}$
